full_text: <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|2.64|> Like many Episcopal churches, here at the cathedral, we use a children's program that's called Godly Play.<|10.84|> <|10.96|> It was created by a wonderful Episcopal priest.<|13.30|> <|14.16|> And Godly Play is a very reverent and yet child-focused, age-appropriate way of teaching the Bible stories and the sacraments.<|25.08|> <|25.08|> It's a wonderful program that we cherish here.<|27.70|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> At the heart of godly play is wondering.<|4.68|> <|5.54|> The storyteller presents a story such as this Christmas story to children and then asks a series of wondering questions.<|13.90|> <|13.90|> For example, I wonder what part of this story is about you.<|21.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Or, and this is the most philosophical question that they ask children, I wonder what part of the story that we could leave out and still have all the story that we need.<|14.48|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> John, the evangelist, the author of the fourth gospel for whom this cathedral is named, leaves out a lot of the Christmas story.<|11.96|> <|14.34|> Much of the popular imagination, much of what I love about Christmas is this creche below the pulpit of<|21.70|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> That's what we think about.<|1.60|> <|1.70|> I think most of us think about when we imagine Christmas.<|4.74|> <|6.14|> John leaves out all of camel.<|4.22|> <|4.76|> Last night, the camel, when we received communion today on the floor,<|9.06|> <|9.58|> last night the creche was in the middle of the carpet.<|12.24|> <|12.40|> We served communion just on the other side of it.<|14.92|> <|15.56|> And Deacon Jack, this was his first Christmas Eve at the cathedral,<|19.92|> <|19.92|> and I had to warn him as he was giving people communion,<|22.98|> <|23.52|> there was a camel nipping at his heel.<|26.16|> <|27.62|> Laughter<|28.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> All of it's gone, all of it's cleared in John's gospel.<|5.28|> <|6.64|> Joseph is nowhere to be seen.<|10.12|> <|11.72|> Mary is not even mentioned.<|14.12|> <|15.34|> And there's not even an infant there.<|19.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Instead, what John gives us as the Christmas message is simply this:<|9.54|> <|9.54|> "And the Word became flesh and dwelt among us."<|15.60|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Flesh here is a fascinating and rich word.<|5.00|> <|6.38|> It literally means all of human nature,<|10.74|> <|10.74|> all flesh and blood,<|13.28|> <|13.28|> every single person who ever has existed<|17.04|> <|17.04|> or ever will exist.<|19.26|> <|19.26|> Somehow this word of God, this presence of God<|23.06|> <|23.06|> is echoing in all of human flesh<|26.80|> <|26.80|> and all of our hearts and souls.<|29.70|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And that for John is what Christmas is all about.<|5.60|> <|5.60|> The meaning of it for John is that the light that is God's presence shines in everyone.<|18.70|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> My favorite part of Christmas is the creche, but I always love Christmas morning here at the cathedral when we read this gospel.<|8.28|> <|8.28|> Because I think what John does is takes us to the outermost edges of the creche scene, the outermost edges of the manger scene where you and I stand or kneel.<|24.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And what John invites us to imagine in the most powerful and personal way is Christ being born in each of us.<|10.40|> <|13.26|> It was a great 14th century story.<|17.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> preacher and member of the Dominican order named Meister Eckhart.<|5.58|> <|7.10|> And Eckhart is a beloved mystic in the Christian tradition, and he was a wonderful preacher.<|12.50|> <|13.72|> Back then in the medieval period, most sermons were in Latin.<|17.14|> <|17.34|> Most of the liturgy was in Latin.<|18.66|> <|18.66|> There were a few exceptions to that.<|20.90|> <|21.08|> Germany in the 14th century, for some reason, actually was a preacher.<|24.70|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And Meister Eckhart, although he preached a lot in Latin,<|2.64|> <|2.64|> he loved to preach sermons in German.<|6.48|> <|6.98|> It's just simple parish churches.<|8.48|> <|8.66|> And his sermons are just lovely.<|10.78|> <|11.90|> And once on a Christmas morning service, just like this,<|16.50|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Meister Eckhart preached a lovely Christmas Day homily.<|4.06|> <|4.16|> You can still find it online in a couple of the Penguin Press books about Eckhart.<|9.24|> <|9.50|> I commend it to you.<|10.90|> <|11.24|> But more importantly, here's what Eckhart said about Christmas.<|14.06|> <|14.06|> He says, basically...<|18.50|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> What matters the most is not that Christ was born centuries ago.<|5.82|> <|6.74|> What matters most is that Christ is born in me and in you.<|12.98|> <|13.94|> That's what matters.<|15.50|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And I think that's the profound invitation of John chapter 1,<|4.36|> <|5.08|> John's retelling of the Christmas story<|8.46|> <|8.46|> from the outermost edges of the manger or the creche.<|12.98|> <|16.10|> Right now we'll have just a brief moment of silence before the creed.<|21.72|> <|22.82|> And during this silence, I invite you to,<|26.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> to wonder and to think about the way in which Christ is being born in your own soul,<|9.18|> <|9.88|> in your own life, in the very details of your life,<|15.16|> <|15.92|> and in the people around you whom you hold most dear.<|18.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Thank you.<|5.66|><|endoftext|>
